diagrams.title: Linear Recursion Flow - Factorial Example
diagrams.description: Shows the step-by-step execution of a linear recursive function (factorial n!) demonstrating how the call stack grows and then unwinds. Each call makes exactly ONE recursive call to itself.
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how linear recursion works by showing the downward phase (building the stack) and upward phase (resolving calls), helping students understand the single recursive call pattern and when the base case stops the recursion.

diagrams.title: Binary Recursion Flow - Fibonacci Example
diagrams.description: Illustrates the binary recursive pattern using Fibonacci sequence, showing how each call makes TWO recursive calls. Displays the call tree structure with overlapping subproblems.
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrate binary recursion where each function calls itself twice, making the call tree structure visible. Helps students understand why binary recursion can be inefficient due to repeated calculations and introduces the concept of memoization as a solution.

diagrams.title: Linear vs Binary Recursion Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing the structural difference between linear recursion (single branch) and binary recursion (binary tree structure), highlighting call patterns and efficiency implications.
diagrams.purpose: Provide a clear visual comparison between linear and binary recursion patterns, reinforcing when to use each type and the trade-offs in terms of call complexity and computational efficiency. Useful for helping students choose the appropriate recursion strategy for different problems.
